event: diagnosed to have tropical chronic pancreatitis
recurrent pain since his diagnosis with tropical pancreatitis
CT scan of the abdomen revealed chronic atrophic calcific pancreatitis
return planned for removal of ductal stones once this acute exacerbation resolved fully
presented to Saint Peter's University Hospital
hospitalized for pain control with narcotics
continuing epigastric pain that gradually increased in intensity
CT scan performed in the emergency department revealed fatty atrophy of both the pancreatic head and uncinate process
pancreatic duct was filled with large calcifications
possible obstruction at the level of the neck
marked upstream ductal dilatation of 1.5 cm
admitted for management of pain secondary to chronic pancreatitis
transferred to the medical intensive care unit
ultrasound of the abdomen: markedly dilated pancreatic duct (1.9 cm)
major papilla of Vater was visualized spontaneously expelling frank pus
evacuation of more than 5 ml of yellow pus
fluid could not be aspirated for culture
cholangiogram revealed a normal biliary tree without stones
pancreatogram showed marked dilatation of the main pancreatic duct
approximately 20 ml of pus was then evacuated during duct manipulation
5-cm-long 5 F stent was placed into the pancreatic duct
acute suppuration of the pancreatic duct
contrast enhanced CT scan of the abdomen
inflammatory changes within the fat surrounding the body and tail of the pancreas consistent with edema
dilatation of the pancreatic duct had diminished
calculus showing distal migration towards the sphincter of Oddi
transferred from the intensive care unit
